author: @newman9940
text: This video will lead you to the conclusion that the two halves of the metal tape case can be separated.   The Lufkin tape case that I was trying to separated and replace the non-metallic tape is SPOT Welded in 8 separate locations.  After prying the metal retaining strap out, which was not too difficult.  I had to cut the 8 spot welds with a Dremel grinder.  I do believe the entire 100' tape could be more easily replaced by threading the new tape in through the hole on the edge on the case and then attaching it to the starting end to the crank arrangement.  Just leave the case welded together.  This entire video will only lead you in the WRONG DIRECTION..  Thumbs Down!!!

author: @newman9940
text: The main reason I looked at this video was to figure out How to get the case apart without bending the pieces.  Unfortunately that part was left out.  By some miracle your case just split into two pieces.  I have a very similar tape with brown vinyl exterior, but the two halves are Stuck tightly together.  No apparent corrosion, but all the prying has only damaged the case, nothing that moved as of yet.

text: Thank you, kind young sir. I am wanting to get A no.100 Lufkin to reel in more smoothly. Before watching this video, I was wondering how to get it apart without destroying the vintage thing. I hope mine comes out as pristine as yours did.

author: @wethepeople1776x
text:
Wait a minute chap... how old inches are? LoL, ‘Merica still uses inches, and used them to get to the moon and back. What other nation can say that? 
LoL, just pulling your leg and having fun.
But honestly, I wish we could use one measurement that we all could agree on. Because it would cutout half the hand tools in my shop, and make projects somewhat easier.

author: @scotttovey
text:
Luke, did you test the inches on that tape with a metal ruler?

Years ago I read an article that stated: over time with use, cloth tapes can stretch which causes the inches to grow longer.

I haven't personally come across that scenario, but I don't do a whole lot of measuring either. Since you have it at hand; it's a good opportunity to test out the claim with your granddads old tape measure to see if there's any truth to it.

author: @stevetobias4890
text: Excellent restoration Luke, something to hand down to your kids.

author: @ThunderDivine
text: Beautiful but its in inches xD

replies.author: @timothygorman7528
replies.text: Still good for when he visits America :P    But I did notice, it's in inches, yes, but very few "graduations" - was it 1/4 or 1/8 inch delineations?

replies.author: @MyNameIsLukey
replies.text: I was thinking about marking the blank side with CM and M because I would be able to use it more often, I didn't want to mess it up though.

replies.author: @timothygorman7528
replies.text:
@MyNameIsLukey Currently it's "cool."  It also holds significant "Sentimental" value, something to be collected.

You actually took a big chance by "cleaning it up" on it's actual monetary value and "collectability" - but you did a GREAT JOB!   

Now you keep it!  Use it very little, don't destroy it, and allow it to remind you of your Grandfather and motivate you as you move along through University and engineering!   

Someday, you might decide to "downsize" and get rid of it, and it does have value!  You actually helped that value out a bit in my estimation :)    It's a really cool thing.

author: @Nuts-Bolts
text: If it is a plasticized fabric tape. Soaking it in hydrogen peroxide over night can bring back much of the brightness. Although this tape looks good as it is, probably because it has remained wound up. Obliviously, its always a good idea to test a small section with hydrogen peroxide first, but generally its safe on most plastic things which have become brown with age. Usually, I keep the solution warm to hot, as it speeds the process up.

replies.author: @mikebond6328
replies.text: A weak solution of hydrogen peroxide like the over the counter stuff?
